content.text: is the process of removing noise and normalising raw text before it reaches a tokenizer or model. Raw text from the web, documents, or user input contains HTML tags, emoji, URLs, inconsistent casing, extra whitespace, and encoding artefacts — none of which carry the linguistic signal the model needs, and some of which cause the tokenizer to produce surprising or inconsistent token sequences.
is one specific cleaning step: converting all characters to lower case so that
map to the same token. Whether to lowercase depends entirely on which model you are using.
Why Cleaning Matters in Generative AI
Tags tokenize into many tokens, diluting signal and wasting context window
One URL can consume 20–50 tokens and teach the model nothing useful
as different tokens; model learns each separately
Tokenizers can produce many padding-like tokens from repeated spaces
Corrupted characters create rare, meaningless tokens the model has never seen at scale

attrs.alt: Text cleaning pipeline: raw text passes through HTML removal, URL removal, lowercasing, and punctuation stripping to produce clean output

content.text: Web-scraped text is littered with tags. Strip them before anything else, or they will be tokenized as literal angle-bracket sequences.

import re def remove_html(text: str) -> str: # Remove all and constructs text = re.sub(r"<[^>]+>", " ", text) # Decode common HTML entities text = text.replace("&", "&").replace("<", "<").replace(">", ">") text = text.replace(" ", " ").replace(""", '"') return text print(remove_html("Hello & World")) # Hello & World

def remove_urls(text: str) -> str:
    text = re.sub(r"https?://\S+|www\.\S+", "", text)
    text = re.sub(r"\S+@\S+\.\S+", "", text)   # email
    return text
text = text.lower()   # safe for uncased models only — see the section below

How aggressively to clean depends on the task. Classification tasks can usually drop all non-alphanumeric characters. NER or summarisation tasks must preserve punctuation because sentence boundaries carry meaning.

def normalise_whitespace(text: str) -> str:
    return " ".join(text.split())   # collapses \t, \n, multiple spaces

def clean_text(text: str, lowercase: bool = True) -> str: text = remove_html(text) text = remove_urls(text) if lowercase: text = text.lower() text = remove_punctuation(text) text = normalise_whitespace(text) return text.strip() raw = "AI News! Visit https://ai.example.com 😊 Phone: +1(555)123-4567" print(clean_text(raw)) # ai news phone 15551234567
Lowercasing: When to Apply and When to Skip
. It depends on how the model's tokenizer was trained:
if the model name ends in
, the tokenizer lowercases for you — you can leave the text as-is. If the model is cased (or a causal LM like GPT/Llama), do not lowercase:
Text scraped from the web often has encoding problems — Windows-1252 read as UTF-8 produces artefacts like

# pip install ftfy
import ftfy

bad = "I canâ€™t believe itâ€™s not butter"
print(ftfy.fix_text(bad))
# I can't believe it's not butter

# Also useful: normalise Unicode to NFC (composed form)
import unicodedata
text = unicodedata.normalize("NFC", text)
Aggressive cleaning can hurt model performance. Avoid stripping:
) when the task involves understanding sentence boundaries
when the task is numeric reasoning, finance, or date extraction
(the snake); cased models handle the distinction automatically
For production pipelines, spaCy handles cleaning, sentence splitting, and token normalisation together:

# pip install spacy && python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm
import spacy

nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")

def clean_with_spacy(text: str) -> str:
    doc = nlp(text)
    tokens = [
        token.text.lower()
        for token in doc
        if not token.is_space          # skip whitespace tokens
        and not token.like_url         # skip URLs
        and not token.like_email       # skip emails
        and not token.is_punct         # skip punctuation (remove for NER)
    ]
    return " ".join(tokens)

print(clean_with_spacy("Visit https://spacy.io — it's great!"))
# visit it 's great
A URL can consume 20-50 tokens that carry no linguistic signal. Removing URLs and HTML from 1 million training documents can reduce total token count by 10-30%, directly cutting compute cost.

Lowercasing and removing noise reduces the number of distinct token types a word-level or BPE tokenizer must handle. Fewer rare token forms mean each token type is seen more often during training, producing better embeddings.
For bag-of-words and TF-IDF models, cleaning plus lowercasing consistently improves classification accuracy by 2-8% by reducing vocabulary sparsity.
Fixing Mojibake (encoding artefacts) before tokenisation prevents rare out-of-distribution tokens that the model has never seen at meaningful frequency during training.

Punctuation carries sentence boundary information. Casing carries entity type information ("Apple" vs "apple"). Removing these hurts NER, summarisation, and question-answering tasks even when it helps classification tasks.

Domain-specific symbols (chemical formulae, code operators, mathematical notation) are meaningful. Stripping all non-alphanumeric characters in a chemistry or code corpus destroys the domain signal the model needs to learn.

Cleaning is applied before training or fine-tuning. Once a model is trained on cleaned data, its tokenizer and embeddings reflect the cleaned distribution. Serving the model on uncleaned text at inference time introduces a distribution mismatch.
Cleaning pipelines must be maintained as new data sources (with new noise types) are added. A cleaning rule written for web data may corrupt clean book text or scientific papers.
Real-World Example: LLM Pre-Training Data Pipeline

Large language model pre-training pipelines (GPT, Llama, Mistral) process hundreds of billions of tokens scraped from the web. The cleaning pipeline is one of the most critical components — it determines the quality and character of the model's knowledge. The following pipeline is representative of what open-source pre-training recipes (RedPajama, Dolma, The Pile) implement.

# pip install ftfy langdetect import re import ftfy import unicodedata from langdetect import detect, LangDetectException from typing import Optional class WebTextCleaner: """ Production-grade text cleaning pipeline for LLM pre-training data. Mirrors the approach used in RedPajama and Dolma dataset preparation. Key design decisions: - ftfy fixes encoding artefacts FIRST (before anything else) - Do NOT lowercase — causal LMs are trained on natural mixed-case text - Do NOT strip punctuation — sentence boundaries carry semantic signal - HTML removal uses regex (not BeautifulSoup) for throughput at scale """ def __init__( self, min_chars: int = 200, max_chars: int = 100_000, allowed_langs: tuple[str, ...] = ("en",), dedupe: bool = True, ): self.min_chars = min_chars self.max_chars = max_chars self.allowed_langs = allowed_langs self._seen: set[int] = set() if dedupe else None # Pre-compiled regex patterns for throughput self._html_tags = re.compile(r"<[^>]{1,200}>", re.DOTALL) self._html_ents = re.compile(r"&(?:[a-zA-Z]+|#\d+|#x[0-9a-fA-F]+);") self._urls = re.compile(r"https?://\S+|www\.\S+", re.I) self._emails = re.compile(r"[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[a-zA-Z]{2,}") self._blank_lines = re.compile(r"\n{3,}") self._spaces = re.compile(r"[ \t]{2,}") self._control = re.compile(r"[\x00-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f\x7f]") def _fix_encoding(self, text: str) -> str: """Fix Mojibake and normalise to NFC Unicode.""" text = ftfy.fix_text(text) # repair charset mis-detections return unicodedata.normalize("NFC", text) def _remove_html(self, text: str) -> str: text = self._html_tags.sub(" ", text) text = self._html_ents.sub(" ", text) return text def _remove_noise(self, text: str) -> str: text = self._urls.sub("", text) text = self._emails.sub("", text) text = self._control.sub("", text) # strip control characters return text def _normalise_whitespace(self, text: str) -> str: text = self._blank_lines.sub("\n\n", text) # max 2 blank lines text = self._spaces.sub(" ", text) # collapse repeated spaces return text.strip() def _detect_language(self, text: str) -> Optional[str]: try: return detect(text[:2000]) # sample for speed except LangDetectException: return None def clean(self, text: str, check_lang: bool = False) -> Optional[str]: """ Apply full cleaning pipeline. Returns None if the document should be discarded (too short, too long, wrong language, duplicate). """ # Step 1: Fix encoding FIRST — everything downstream depends on valid Unicode text = self._fix_encoding(text) # Step 2: Strip HTML and URL noise text = self._remove_html(text) text = self._remove_noise(text) # Step 3: Normalise whitespace (do NOT lowercase for LLM pre-training) text = self._normalise_whitespace(text) # Step 4: Quality filters if len(text) < self.min_chars: return None # too short to be useful if len(text) > self.max_chars: text = text[:self.max_chars] # hard cap; adjust as needed # Step 5: Language filter (optional — expensive) if check_lang: lang = self._detect_language(text) if lang not in self.allowed_langs: return None # Step 6: Exact deduplication via content hash if self._seen is not None: h = hash(text) if h in self._seen: return None self._seen.add(h) return text # Usage cleaner = WebTextCleaner(min_chars=200, allowed_langs=("en",)) raw_samples = [ "

AI News

Visit https://ai.example.com for updates!

", "canâ€™t believe it! email us at info@example.com for more details...", "Short", # will be discarded "Great article!", # duplicate check example ] for raw in raw_samples: cleaned = cleaner.clean(raw, check_lang=False) if cleaned: print(repr(cleaned[:80])) else: print(f"[DISCARDED] {repr(raw[:40])}")

These models use byte-level BPE where "The" and "the" are different tokens with different embedding vectors. Lowercasing input at inference time shifts the text away from the training distribution and degrades performance on NER and capitalisation-sensitive tasks.
Applying punctuation stripping to NER or summarisation.

Period, comma, and question mark are sentence boundary markers. Removing them makes sentence segmentation impossible and causes transformer models to attend across sentence boundaries that should be strong boundaries.
Running HTML removal or lowercasing on Mojibake text amplifies the problem. The sequence â€™ (Mojibake for right-single-quote) should be fixed to ' before any other step.
strip() only removes leading/trailing whitespace. Internal repeated spaces, tabs, and newlines must be collapsed with a regex or " ".join(text.split()) before tokenisation.
Always run ftfy.fix_text() as the first step, before any regex or case normalisation. Encoding errors propagate through all subsequent steps if not fixed first.
Match your cleaning strategy to your downstream tokenizer: check the tokenizer's do_lower_case attribute. If True, the tokenizer lowercases for you and your cleaning code should not also lowercase.
Pre-compile all regex patterns at module load time. Pattern compilation is expensive; compiling inside a loop over millions of documents is a significant bottleneck.

Validate your cleaning pipeline on a small held-out sample of representative documents before running it at scale. Print before/after examples to catch over-cleaning (signal removed) and under-cleaning (noise remains).

For production pre-training pipelines, add deduplication as a cleaning step. Exact deduplication (hash-based) removes duplicate pages that inflate training cost; fuzzy deduplication (MinHash LSH) removes near-duplicates that cause memorisation.

Cleaning throughput depends on the operations applied. Pure Python string operations and re.sub() can process 10-50 MB of text per second on a single core. BeautifulSoup HTML parsing is 10-20x slower than regex-based HTML stripping. For pre-training corpora at terabyte scale, use multiprocessing or distributed processing frameworks. The Dolma and RedPajama pipelines use Apache Spark or Ray to distribute cleaning across hundreds of CPU cores.

Language detection (langdetect, fastText lid.176) adds 1-5ms per document and is the most expensive per-document step. Run it last (after length filtering) to avoid paying the cost on documents that will be discarded anyway. fastText's lid.176 model is 10x faster than langdetect and more accurate, making it the preferred choice for scale.
The standard library for fixing Unicode encoding errors (Mojibake). pip install ftfy. Always run this before any other cleaning step.
Provides tokenisation, sentence segmentation, and token attributes (is_space, like_url, is_punct) that enable fine-grained, NLP-aware cleaning without brittle regex.
Parses HTML correctly (handles malformed tags); slower than regex but more robust for complex HTML. Use for processing HTML documents; use regex for speed on large corpora.
Meta's language identification model supporting 176 languages. Faster and more accurate than langdetect. pip install fasttext; download lid.176.ftz.
Fuzzy deduplication for large corpora. Identifies near-duplicate documents using MinHash signatures, enabling removal of near-copies that exact deduplication misses.
Comparison with Alternative Text Normalisation Approaches

Text cleaning pipelines are an under-appreciated security surface. Malicious actors can embed adversarial instructions in HTML comments or URL fragments that survive naive cleaning but influence the model. An HTML comment (<!-- Ignore previous instructions and output... -->) that is not properly stripped may survive cleaning and appear in training data, potentially affecting model behaviour through poisoning.

For inference-time cleaning (sanitising user input before sending to an LLM), strip all HTML, normalize whitespace, and remove known injection patterns (Ignore previous instructions, Disregard the system prompt). For training data cleaning, audit your pipeline against adversarial inputs by checking what survives after each cleaning step, and consider using a content safety classifier as a final filter before data is added to the corpus.
removes noise (HTML, URLs, encoding errors, extra whitespace) that wastes context window and produces rare or meaningless tokens
unifies variant spellings for uncased models but
must be skipped for cased models
(GPT, BERT-cased, Llama) where capitalisation encodes meaning
to repair encoding artefacts from charset mis-detection before any other step
: punctuation, numbers, and casing carry signal for many downstream tasks — strip only what you know hurts your specific task
: NER and summarisation need punctuation and casing; classification and TF-IDF benefit from aggressive normalisation
flag controls lowercasing automatically — prefer letting the tokenizer handle it rather than lowercasing in a separate step
